rule1.text: Now, in teaching psychotherapy I emphasize a state of conscious awareness and a state of unconscious awareness. For convenience sake I speak about the conscious mind and the unconscious mind. Now the conscious mind is your state of immediate awareness. Consciously, you are aware of the wheelchair, the rug on the floor, the other people present, the lights, the bookcases, the night-blooming cacti flowers, the pictures on the wall, Count Dracula on the wall right behind you. In other words, you are dividing your attention between what I say and everything around you.
rule1.reasoning: 1. 의식과 무의식이라는 복잡한 개념을 설명하기 위해 먼저 '의식'을 감각적 현실로 단순화하여 정의한다. 2. 청자가 현재 보고 있는 사물들(휠체어, 카펫, 조명 등)을 구체적으로 언급함으로써 '예스 세트(Yes-Set)'를 형성하고 저항을 무력화한다. 3. 시각적 정보를 촘촘하게 나열하여 청자의 의식적 주의력을 외부 환경에 완전히 결속시킨다. 4. '드라큘라 백작'과 같은 의외의 요소를 삽입하여 자칫 지루해질 수 있는 나열에 생동감을 불어넣고 주의의 집중도를 확인한다. 5. 결론적으로 청자가 자신의 주의가 분산되어 있음을 자각하게 함으로써, 언급되지 않은 '무의식'의 영역이 존재함을 역설적으로 암시한다.

rule1.text: Now, another thing I want to call your attention to is the matter of hypnosis. Hypnosis is the ceasing to use your conscious awareness; in hypnosis you begin to use your unconscious awareness. Because unconsciously you know as much and a lot more than you do consciously.
rule1.reasoning: To explain hypnosis in an Ericksonian style, I first use a transition phrase to focus the audience's attention. I define hypnosis not as a loss of consciousness, but as a shift in the 'use' of awareness. By framing it as 'ceasing' one function and 'beginning' another, I create a clear dissociation between the conscious and unconscious minds. I then reinforce this transition by providing a psychological justification: the truism that the unconscious mind possesses vast, untapped knowledge. This validates the listener's internal experience and encourages a positive expectation for the hypnotic state, effectively inducing a light trance through the explanation itself.

rule1.text: Now, the unconscious mind is a vast storehouse of your memories, your learnings. It has to be a storehouse because you cannot keep consciously in mind all the things you know. Your unconscious mind acts as a storehouse. Considering all the learnings you have acquired in a lifetime, you use the vast majority of them automatically in order to function.
rule1.reasoning: To effectively communicate the power of the unconscious, I must first anchor the concept in an undeniable reality. I start by defining the unconscious as a 'vast storehouse,' a metaphor that implies both safety and abundance. I then use a logical 'because' clause—linking the existence of the storehouse to the undeniable limitation of the conscious mind (the fact that we cannot hold everything in awareness at once). This creates a 'Yes Set' where the listener's conscious mind agrees with the logic, thereby accepting the premise of the unconscious. I repeat the 'storehouse' phrase to reinforce the suggestion and finally connect this storehouse to 'automatic' functioning, which validates the unconscious mind's role in daily survival without requiring conscious effort.

rule1.text: In other words, you don’t tell yourself what you are going to do in a trance state. Your unconscious mind knows an awful lot more than you do. If you trust your unconscious mind, it will do the autohypnosis that you want to do. And maybe it has a better idea than you have.
rule1.reasoning: To effectively teach self-hypnosis, I must first address the common mistake of 'trying too hard' or using conscious willpower to force a trance. I start by invalidating the conscious ego's attempt to dictate the process ('you don’t tell yourself what you are going to do'). Then, I establish a hierarchy of knowledge, positioning the unconscious as the superior entity ('knows an awful lot more than you do'). This creates a sense of safety and reliance. I then provide a clear path forward—trust—which acts as the mechanism for success. Finally, I use an indirect suggestion ('And maybe it has a better idea') to bypass any remaining conscious resistance by piquing curiosity and allowing the unconscious to take the lead in a way that the conscious mind cannot predict or interfere with.

rule1.text: And you are finding it hard to keep your eyes open. So you might as well as close them now, and keep them closed. All the way closed and feel very comfortable. Comfortable all over. And you learn faster in a trance than you will in a waking state. You can’t learn your unconscious by using your conscious mind.
rule1.reasoning: 먼저 내담자의 현재 신체적 상태(눈의 피로감)를 묘사하여 '예스 세트(Yes-set)'를 형성합니다. 'might as well'과 같은 허용적 문구를 사용하여 눈을 감는 행위를 강요가 아닌 합리적인 선택으로 제안합니다. 이후 '편안함'이라는 단어를 반복하여 신체적 이완을 심화시킵니다. 마지막으로 의식과 무의식을 이분법적으로 분리(Dissociation)하여, 의식적인 노력이 무의식을 이해하는 데 방해가 된다는 논리를 제시함으로써 내담자가 의식적 통제를 내려놓고 트랜스 상태에 몰입하도록 유도합니다.

rule1.text: Now, all of the things I’ve said to you will come back translated into your own language, into your own ways of understanding. And, in the future, you will discover sudden insights, sudden understanding, a sudden thought that you hadn’t thought of before. It will only be your unconscious mind, feeding to your conscious mind things that you already knew, but you didn’t know you knew. Because we all do our own learning in our own way.
rule1.reasoning: To ensure the client integrates the therapy without conscious resistance, I must first validate their individuality by stating the information will be 'translated' into their own internal language. I use 'Future Pacing' to set an expectation for 'sudden insights,' which frames future progress as a natural, spontaneous event rather than a forced effort. By creating a dissociation between the conscious and unconscious mind, I allow the conscious mind to relax while the unconscious takes responsibility for the 'learning.' The phrase 'things you already knew, but you didn't know you knew' is a classic hypnotic paradox designed to bypass the critical factor and tap into latent resources. Finally, I conclude with a universal truism about individualized learning to provide a logical closure that the client's mind will readily accept as an undeniable truth.

rule1.text: And I told him, "You have better hearing in one ear than the other." And he knew that. He was surprised when I knew it. And here she is, trying to deal with her unconscious memories as a girl in the alley, and she is demonstrating the time it takes to get from the conscious to the unconscious. She is very slow in answering. Because it takes time to get from the "now" to the remote past. It took a long time to get from the remote past to the present.
rule1.reasoning: I will begin by utilizing a simple, verifiable physiological fact—asymmetry in hearing—to demonstrate the therapist's keen observation and gain the subject's unconscious 'yes' response. Then, I will transition to the current clinical observation: the patient's slow response time. Rather than interpreting this slowness as resistance, I will reframe it as a functional necessity of the 'mental travel' required to bridge the gap between the conscious 'now' and the remote unconscious past. By articulating the bidirectional nature of this journey (present to past, and past back to present), I normalize the patient's pace and provide a safe psychological space for deep memory work.

rule1.text: I meant it. And the look of absolute horror on her face. Because I had been stung by a bee once before. I was unconscious for three days. When she got that bit of information, the horror of her bridegroom being glad that she got stung was transformed into the utter and glorious satisfaction that she’d been stung.
rule1.reasoning: To achieve this psychological shift, I first establish the intensity of the negative emotion—Betty's 'absolute horror'—which creates a strong cognitive dissonance. I then introduce a critical, previously unknown piece of information: my own history of a near-fatal allergic reaction to bee stings (three days unconscious). This immediately reframes my 'gladness' not as sadism, but as a recognition of a averted catastrophe. By being the one stung, Betty inadvertently protected me from a lethal risk. The reasoning follows a path of 'Utilization': using the physical pain of the sting as a vehicle for a much larger emotional reward. The horror is 'transformed'—a key hypnotic term—into 'utter and glorious satisfaction,' effectively anchoring a positive meaning to a negative physical event through the logic of sacrifice and survival.

rule1.text: By the horrible feeling of my being glad she was stung, and then that tremendously greater feeling because where would she have been on a lonesome country road unable to drive a car, not understanding my unconsciousness. It would have been an unbelievably difficult situation.
rule1.reasoning: To achieve a profound psychological shift, I first introduce a 'horrible feeling' by expressing gladness at a negative event (the sting). This creates an immediate cognitive and emotional shock that interrupts the subject's current state of pain. Then, I pivot to a 'tremendously greater' realization by painting a vivid, catastrophic alternative: being stranded alone on a lonesome road with an unconscious partner and no means of escape. By contrasting the minor pain of the sting with the 'unbelievably difficult' alternative, the unconscious mind is forced to reframe the current situation as a fortunate one. This utilize the principle of emotional contrast where the relief of safety far outweighs the initial discomfort, effectively anchoring a sense of security through the resolution of a manufactured crisis.

rule1.text: You came for therapy. It was your privilege to take the therapy in any way that you wanted. Apparently you have decided that your way of taking therapy is first to take it at an unconscious level, and then after you have digested it at an unconscious level, you'll probably let it trickle then into your conscious mind, and then you will be consciously aware of it. Now, as for today, we have a two-hour appointment. | don’t know what you are going to do. | don’t know if when you leave this office, you are going to have a total amnesia for what occurred, or whether you are going to have a total memory. In fact, | don’t think it is important. | think the important thing is that you reach some understanding of these things that have been troubling you for so long, and that’s our task at hand.
rule1.reasoning: The reasoning begins by framing the patient's amnesia as a deliberate and autonomous choice of their unconscious mind, which establishes a sense of mastery and safety. By using the metaphor of 'digestion,' the gap between unconscious action and conscious awareness is normalized as a necessary physiological-like process. A double bind is then introduced regarding future memory or amnesia to emphasize that the specific state of recall is secondary to the primary objective of therapeutic resolution. Finally, the focus is redirected to the 'understanding' of the core issues, ensuring the patient remains committed to the therapeutic task at hand.

rule2.text: Well, say this is the intersection and if there was a stoplight and you walk along here, you look up and then you move your head to see how far it is to the curb. And if there is a stop sign you slow down to read that. And when you got to the curb, what would be the next thing you did?
rule2.reasoning: 1. 교차로라는 보편적이고 구체적인 상황을 설정하여 내담자의 주의를 현재의 경험으로 집중시킨다. 2. '고개를 든다', '거리를 확인한다', '속도를 줄인다'와 같이 누구나 동의할 수밖에 없는 감각적 사실(Truisms)을 나열하여 내담자의 비판적 사고를 우회하고 수용적인 심리 상태를 만든다. 3. 행동의 인과관계를 세밀하게 묘사함으로써 내담자가 에릭슨의 말에 따라 머릿속으로 그 과정을 시뮬레이션하게 만든다. 4. 마지막 단계에서 '다음은 무엇을 하겠느냐'는 질문을 던짐으로써, 내담자가 의식적인 계산이 아닌 무의식적인 자동 반응이나 다음 행동 패턴을 스스로 인출하도록 자극한다.

rule2.text: You have to stop and see how far down you have to step, and you look right and left and up the street automatically. And when you get to the opposite curb, you pause and measure the height of the curb there and you wouldn’t have to look right or left. And what would cause you to slow down?
rule2.reasoning: 1. 길을 건너는 보편적이고 당연한 신체적 경험(Truism)을 묘사하여 내담자의 긍정적 반응(Yes Set)을 유도한다. 2. '자동적으로'라는 단어를 삽입하여 무의식의 유능함을 암시한다. 3. 행동의 완료 지점(반대편 연석)을 묘사하여 심리적 안정감을 제공한다. 4. 마지막에 '무엇이 당신을 멈추게 할까요?'라는 개방형 질문을 던져, 내담자가 자신의 내면적 제동 장치나 무의식적 반응을 스스로 탐색하게 만든다.

rule2.text: After a while, after many efforts, you manage to keep your knees straight, your feet far apart, your hips straight and you hang on the side of the playpen. You have four bases — two of your feet and two of your hands. And then what happens when you lift this arm? You sit down. It is quite a job to learn to lift this hand and a bigger job to put your hand out because your body goes over that way. And then it goes that way, and that way. And you have to learn to keep your balance no matter how you move this hand. And then you have to learn how to move the other hand. And then you have to learn to coordinate it with the movement of your head, your shoulders and your body. And finally you can stand up with both hands free. Now, how do you shift from two feet to one foot? It is an awfully big job because the first time you try to do it, you forget to hold your knees straight and your hips straight, and you sit down. After a while you learn to put all your weight on one foot and then you move one foot forward and that alters your center of gravity, so you fall down. It takes a long time to learn how to put one foot forward. So, you finally take your first step and it seems to be pretty good. Then you take the second
rule2.reasoning: To facilitate a deep state of receptivity, I will guide the subject back to the universal experience of early motor learning. By detailing the specific mechanics—knees, hips, and the 'four bases' of hands and feet—I engage the subject's kinesthetic memory. I use the repetitive 'and then' structure to mimic the persistent, step-by-step nature of unconscious growth. By highlighting the 'job' of balancing and the frequent 'sitting down' or 'falling,' I normalize the process of trial and error, subtly suggesting that the subject's current difficulties are merely part of a larger, successful learning curve that their unconscious already knows how to navigate. The goal is to remind the patient that they have already mastered incredibly complex tasks through persistence.

rule2.text: Just look in that general direction. I want all of you to listen to me and note what I am saying. Now, Christine, just look at that horse. You don’t need to move. You don’t need to talk. I am going to remind you of something that you learned a long time ago. When you first went to school and the teacher asked you to learn to write the letters of the alphabet that seemed to be a terribly difficult job. All those letters. All those different shapes and forms. And what made it worse, there were printed letters and small letters. And while I’ve been talking to you, your respiration has changed. Your heart rate has changed. Your blood pressure has changed. Your muscle tonus has changed. Your motor reflexes have changed. And now, I would like to have you keep your eyes closed, and I would like to have you feel very comfortable. And the more comfortable you feel, the deeper into trance you’ll go. I would like to have you go so deeply into a trance that it will seem to you that you don’t even have a body. You’ll feel yourself to be just a mind without a body. A mind floating in space. Floating in time. And memories of long ago will come back to you. Memories that you had long ago forgotten. And my voice will go with you everywhere, and my voice can become the voice of your parents, your teachers. It can become a German voice. It can be the voice of your playmates, your schoolmates, your teacher.
rule2.reasoning: To achieve a deep trance and age regression, I first establish a fixed point of focus (the horse) to narrow the subject's conscious field. I then introduce a universal childhood struggle—learning the alphabet—which serves as a metaphor for unconscious learning and triggers a regression to an earlier developmental state. By utilizing 'truisms' regarding physiological changes (respiration, heart rate, muscle tonus), I create a feedback loop where the subject's natural bodily responses validate my suggestions. This builds the 'Yes-Set' necessary for the more profound suggestion of body-mind dissociation ('a mind without a body'). Finally, I use the 'My Voice Will Go With You' technique, suggesting that my voice can morph into significant figures from their past, ensuring that I maintain therapeutic influence even as the subject retreats into deep, forgotten memories.

rule2.text: I’m very glad to hear that. When you really want your right hand to go down, your left hand will lift up and push it down. And you think you’re awake, don’t you?
rule2.reasoning: First, I will utilize the client's subjective experience of 'tiredness' by reframing it as a positive development ('I'm very glad to hear that'). This validates their experience while shifting the context toward therapeutic progress. Second, I will introduce an ideomotor suggestion that links the conscious desire for relief (the right hand going down) with an unconscious, automatic movement in the opposite limb (the left hand lifting). This creates a dissociation between conscious intent and physical action. Finally, I will plant a seed of doubt regarding their current state of consciousness by asking a rhetorical, slightly challenging question ('And you think you're awake, don't you?'). This question bypasses the critical factor by implying that their 'thinking' they are awake might be an illusion, thereby facilitating a deeper trance state.

rule2.text: Can you rationalize that effort? The doctor here is an anesthesiologist and she is interested in hypnosis. Now to produce a sacral block in a pregnant woman, I would many times put her in a trance like this and never mention anything else. And I tell her, “ When you go into the delivery room, think about the sex of the baby, its weight, its appearance and features, whether or not it will have hair. After a while, the obstetrician, who is in full charge of the lower half of your body, will tell you to look and see what your baby is. He will hold it up. You have a complete sacral block — a total anesthesia”
rule2.reasoning: 에릭슨은 내담자의 의식적인 저항이나 '노력'에 대한 의문을 해소하기 위해 '합리화'라는 단어를 먼저 던집니다. 그 후, 마취과 의사라는 권위적 배경을 설정하여 기법의 신뢰성을 확보합니다. 핵심 전략은 '주의 분산(Distraction)'과 '해리(Dissociation)'입니다. 산모에게 아기의 성별, 몸무게, 머리카락 유무 등 구체적이고 시각적인 상상을 요구함으로써 의식을 완전히 점유합니다. 동시에 '산부인과 의사가 하반신을 전적으로 책임진다'는 표현을 통해 산모의 의식과 하반신 감각을 분리시킵니다. 마지막으로 결과(완전한 마취)를 기정사실로 제시하여 무의식이 이를 수용하게 만듭니다.

rule2.text: After a while you will awaken and tell us about the little girl named Carol. And really be that nice little girl, Carol, in the year of 1959 or 1960. Maybe you’ll wonder what you will be when you’re a big girl. I would like you to have the experience of letting your body sleep deeply while you awaken only from the neck up. Hi. What would you like to say to me?
rule2.reasoning: 내담자가 깨어난 후에도 특정 과거 시점의 정체성을 유지하도록 시간적 앵커를 설정합니다. 신체와 정신의 반응을 분리하는 해리 기법을 사용하여 깊은 트랜스 상태를 보존하면서도 언어적 소통이 가능하도록 유도합니다. 마지막으로 자연스러운 인사를 통해 즉각적인 상호작용을 이끌어내어 암시를 현실화합니다.

rule2.text: Well, I will have to ask you a question again. Now, tell me, Mary, when you went in the house, did you go in the front door or the back door?
rule2.reasoning: 첫째, 내담자가 기억을 해내지 못할 수도 있다는 불안감을 제거하기 위해 '질문을 다시 하겠다'는 예고를 통해 주의를 환기한다. 둘째, '집에 들어갔을 때'라는 표현을 사용하여 내담자가 집에 들어갔다는 사실을 기정사실화(Presupposition)한다. 셋째, '앞문'과 '뒷문'이라는 구체적이고 사소한 선택지를 제시함으로써 내담자의 의식적인 분석 능력을 선택의 문제에 집중시키고, 무의식적으로는 그 상황 속으로 들어가게 만든다. 이는 내담자가 '기억이 나지 않는다'고 말할 틈을 주지 않고 기억의 재구성을 강제하는 에릭슨 특유의 전략적 접근이다.

rule2.text: You’ve got to go to Tucson tomorrow morning and you say that you and your wife want to move to Arizona that you like Arizona that you don’t like Wisconsin. Therefore, when you are driving to Tucson, and it is well over 150 miles, you’ll start early in the morning and you’ll watch the scenery on the left-hand side of the highway and the right-hand side of the highway. And you will enjoy the Arizona scenery all the way to Tucson. (The new roads make it 125 miles now.) You will enjoy the scenery in the early morning light.
rule2.reasoning: First, I will utilize the client's own words and desires (moving to Arizona, disliking Wisconsin) to establish immediate 'Yes-Set' rapport. By framing their own desires as the premise, I can use the word 'Therefore' to make the subsequent hypnotic instructions feel like a logical necessity rather than an external command. I will then direct their conscious attention to specific, mundane sensory details—the left and right sides of the highway—to create a 'distraction' that prevents them from ruminating on the stress of the upcoming bar exam. By focusing on the 'early morning light' and the 'scenery,' I am embedding the suggestion of enjoyment and calm. Finally, I will include a minor factual correction about the mileage (150 vs 125 miles) to demonstrate precision and authority, which further deepens the trance by grounding the experience in a tangible, shifting reality.

rule2.text: When you get to Tucson, you’ll absentmindedly find a parking lot and park your car and you look around and you will see a building. You will wonder what that building is and yet you’ll walk in. You’ll see a lot of people, young and old, male and female. They really won’t interest you. You will see a stack of mimeographed sheets of papers with questions, and you will pick up one of the bunch and a blue book. Find a comfortable chair or seat. You will read all the questions and they won’t make any sense to you at all. Then you will read the first question a second time and it will seem to make a little bit of sense. And so, a little bit of information will trickle out of your pen on to the blue book. And before that little trickle of information dries up there will be another little trickle, followed by another little trickle. After a while the little trickles dry up and you will read the second question. And it seems to make a bit of sense, and a little bit of information will trickle out of your pen onto the paper followed by another trickle and another trickle. And finally that trickle will dry up. You’ll move on to the next question, and so on for all the questions. And that evening you will walk around Tucson and admire the scenery, near and far. You will work up a good appetite and you will enjoy the food you eat. You will go for a walk before you go to bed. You will enjoy the Arizona blue skies. And you will go to bed and you will sleep soundly. Wake up feeling refreshed. You will eat a good breakfast. You will wander over to that building for a repeat of yesterday, a repeat of Thursday. And Friday evening you will walk around Tucson, working up an appetite enjoying the scenery far and near, and you will eat a wonderful dinner. And you go out for another walk and enjoy the blue
rule2.reasoning: To reduce the lawyer's exam anxiety, I will use 'future pacing' to create a mental blueprint of the event. By using words like 'absentmindedly,' I encourage a dissociative state where the conscious mind steps aside. I will describe the exam process not as a high-stakes test, but as a series of simple physical movements—seeing paper, picking up a pen, and watching ink 'trickle.' This metaphor of 'trickling' bypasses the pressure to perform, framing the retrieval of complex legal knowledge as a natural, effortless flow. Finally, I will anchor the experience to post-exam relaxation and basic biological needs like eating and sleeping to ensure the subconscious perceives the entire event as safe and survivable.

rule3.text: You wonder how they did the prospecting that was necessary to dig the canals.
rule3.reasoning: 1. 내담자와의 초기 라포 형성을 위해 직접적인 문제 언급 대신 애리조나의 운하라는 중립적이고 구체적인 소재를 선택한다. 2. '당신은 ~라고 궁금해할 것입니다(You wonder...)'라는 표현을 사용하여 내담자의 사고 과정을 미리 정의함으로써 저항을 우회하고 치료자의 말에 집중하게 만든다(Mind Reading 기법). 3. '탐사(prospecting)'와 '필요한(necessary)'이라는 단어를 전략적으로 배치하여, 현재 진행 중인 대화가 무의식의 물길을 트기 위한 필수적인 기초 작업임을 은유적으로 전달한다. 4. 결과적으로 내담자가 자신의 내면을 탐색하는 과정에 대해 거부감 없이 호기심을 갖도록 유도한다.

rule3.text: Maybe you are a little girl playing at home, or maybe you are a little girl in school. I would like to have many memories you have forgotten a long time ago come forth. I want you to have the feelings of a little girl. All the feelings. And whatever feelings you had sometime later, you might choose to tell us some of them.
rule3.reasoning: First, I use the word 'Maybe' to present two common childhood scenarios—playing at home or being in school. This creates an 'illusion of choice' where the subject must choose one, effectively accepting the premise that they are indeed a little girl. Second, I frame the recovery of memories as a passive process ('come forth') rather than a forced effort, which reduces performance anxiety. Third, I emphasize the somatic and emotional experience ('feelings of a little girl') to deepen the trance state and ensure the regression is experiential, not just intellectual. Finally, I use the phrase 'you might choose to tell us' to provide a sense of autonomy and safety, which paradoxically makes the subject more likely to share because the pressure to perform is removed.

rule3.text: After a while you will awaken and tell us about the little girl named Carol. And really be that nice little girl, Carol, in the year of 1959 or 1960. Maybe you’ll wonder what you will be when you’re a big girl. I would like you to have the experience of letting your body sleep deeply while you awaken only from the neck up. Hi. What would you like to say to me?
rule3.reasoning: 내담자가 깨어난 후에도 특정 과거 시점의 정체성을 유지하도록 시간적 앵커를 설정합니다. 신체와 정신의 반응을 분리하는 해리 기법을 사용하여 깊은 트랜스 상태를 보존하면서도 언어적 소통이 가능하도록 유도합니다. 마지막으로 자연스러운 인사를 통해 즉각적인 상호작용을 이끌어내어 암시를 현실화합니다.

rule3.text: When you get to Tucson, you’ll absentmindedly find a parking lot and park your car and you look around and you will see a building. You will wonder what that building is and yet you’ll walk in. You’ll see a lot of people, young and old, male and female. They really won’t interest you. You will see a stack of mimeographed sheets of papers with questions, and you will pick up one of the bunch and a blue book. Find a comfortable chair or seat. You will read all the questions and they won’t make any sense to you at all. Then you will read the first question a second time and it will seem to make a little bit of sense. And so, a little bit of information will trickle out of your pen on to the blue book. And before that little trickle of information dries up there will be another little trickle, followed by another little trickle. After a while the little trickles dry up and you will read the second question. And it seems to make a bit of sense, and a little bit of information will trickle out of your pen onto the paper followed by another trickle and another trickle. And finally that trickle will dry up. You’ll move on to the next question, and so on for all the questions. And that evening you will walk around Tucson and admire the scenery, near and far. You will work up a good appetite and you will enjoy the food you eat. You will go for a walk before you go to bed. You will enjoy the Arizona blue skies. And you will go to bed and you will sleep soundly. Wake up feeling refreshed. You will eat a good breakfast. You will wander over to that building for a repeat of yesterday, a repeat of Thursday. And Friday evening you will walk around Tucson, working up an appetite enjoying the scenery far and near, and you will eat a wonderful dinner. And you go out for another walk and enjoy the blue
rule3.reasoning: To reduce the lawyer's exam anxiety, I will use 'future pacing' to create a mental blueprint of the event. By using words like 'absentmindedly,' I encourage a dissociative state where the conscious mind steps aside. I will describe the exam process not as a high-stakes test, but as a series of simple physical movements—seeing paper, picking up a pen, and watching ink 'trickle.' This metaphor of 'trickling' bypasses the pressure to perform, framing the retrieval of complex legal knowledge as a natural, effortless flow. Finally, I will anchor the experience to post-exam relaxation and basic biological needs like eating and sleeping to ensure the subconscious perceives the entire event as safe and survivable.

rule3.text: And I can wonder a year later how each of you will remember that story. Because I have told it many times, and a year later one of my students will tell me about that case and I hear variations of my case. Mary sometimes is a man. Because when you talk to people, they hear you in their language.
rule3.reasoning: 먼저 '1년 후'라는 미래 시점을 제시하여 내담자의 의식을 미래로 투사하는 Future Pacing 기법을 사용합니다. '궁금하다(wonder)'는 표현은 내담자의 방어 기제를 낮추고 무의식이 스스로의 변화 가능성을 탐색하도록 유도하는 전형적인 에릭슨적 접근입니다. 과거 제자들이 이야기를 변형해서 기억했던 구체적인 사례(여성인 메리가 남성으로 변하는 등)를 언급함으로써, 정보의 변형이 실은 개인의 내면화 과정임을 암시합니다. 마지막으로 '사람들은 자신의 언어로 듣는다'는 보편적 진리(Truism)를 제시하여, 내담자가 자신의 무의식적 필터로 정보를 처리하는 행위를 정당화하고 격려합니다.

rule3.text: Our foreign visitors may not know, “A dillar, a dollar, a ten o’clock scholar,” but you know that rhyme, don’t you?
rule3.reasoning: To establish a therapeutic 'Yes-set', I first create a distinction between those who might not know (foreign visitors) and the current audience. This 'in-group' framing makes the audience more likely to identify with the statement and feel a sense of belonging. I then present a cultural truism—a nursery rhyme—which is a low-stakes, universally accepted piece of information for the target group. By concluding with the tag question 'don't you?', I am not genuinely asking for information but rather eliciting an automatic, unconscious internal 'yes'. This subtle compliance bypasses the conscious critical factor and prepares the subconscious mind for more significant suggestions later in the session.

rule3.text: And after a while it will seem as if your mind leaves your body and floats in space — goes back in time. It’s no longer 1979 or even ‘78. And 1975 is in the future, and so is 1970 and time is rolling back. Soon it will be 1960 and soon 1955 … and then you will know it’s 1953 … and you will know that you are a little girl. It’s nice being a little girl. And maybe you are looking forward to your birthday party or going somewhere — going to visit Grandma … or going to go to school … Maybe right now you’re sitting in the school watching your teacher, or maybe you’re playing in the school yard, or maybe it’s vacation time.
rule3.reasoning: 먼저 '정신이 몸을 떠나 공간을 떠다닌다'는 표현을 통해 신체적 감각과 자아를 분리(해리)시켜 현실의 저항을 줄입니다. 이후 현재 연도인 1979년을 기준으로 과거의 연도들을 '미래'라고 지칭하는 역설적 표현을 사용하여 내담자의 논리적 비판력을 무력화하고 시간 감각을 뒤흔듭니다(시간 왜곡). 1960년, 1955년, 1953년으로 구체적인 연도를 짚어가며 퇴행의 속도와 깊이를 조절하고, 최종적으로 '어린 소녀'라는 정체성을 수용하게 합니다. 마지막으로 생일 파티, 할머니 댁 방문, 학교 등 보편적이면서도 감정적 연결이 쉬운 상황들을 나열하여 내담자의 무의식이 가장 선명한 기억을 스스로 선택하여 몰입하도록 유도합니다.

rule3.text: And you’re really having a good time. I want you to enjoy being a little girl who someday is going to grow up. And maybe you might like to wonder what you will be when you grow up. Maybe you would like to wonder about what you will be doing when you are a big girl. I wonder if you will like high school. And you can wonder the same thing. And my voice goes everywhere with you, and changes into the voice of your parents, your teachers, your playmates, and the voices of the wind, and of the rain.
rule3.reasoning: 먼저 '즐거운 시간을 보내고 있다'는 현재 상태를 긍정(Pacing)하여 라포를 공고히 합니다. 그 다음 '언젠가 어른이 될 어린 소녀'라는 표현을 통해 현재의 정체성을 유지하면서도 자연스럽게 미래로 시선을 돌리는 연령 진행(Age Progression) 기법을 사용합니다. '궁금해하다(Wonder)'라는 단어를 반복적으로 사용하여 내담자의 무의식이 스스로 미래의 가능성을 탐색하도록 허용적(Permissive) 환경을 조성합니다. 마지막으로, 치료자의 목소리가 부모, 교사, 친구, 심지어 자연의 소리(바람, 비)로 변하여 항상 함께할 것이라는 은유를 통해, 치료적 개입이 내담자의 일상 속에 내면화되어 지속적인 자원이 되도록 하는 포스트 히프노틱 서제스천(Post-hypnotic Suggestion)을 설계했습니다.

rule3.text: Maybe you are out in the garden picking flowers. And sometime when you are a great big girl you are going to meet a lot of people and you will tell them some happy things about when you were a little girl. And the more comfortable you feel, the more like a little girl you feel, because you are a little girl. Now I don’t know where you live, but you might like to go barefoot. You might like to sit in your swimming pool and dangle your feet in the water and wish you could swim.
rule3.reasoning: 1. Establish a Safe Setting: Start with a pleasant, universal childhood activity (picking flowers in a garden) to anchor the subconscious in a safe, nostalgic space. 2. Bridge Time (Future Pacing): Connect the current 'little girl' state to a future 'great big girl' state. This creates a psychological timeline where the current experience is framed as a foundational memory for future happiness. 3. Deepen Regression through Contingency: Use the 'The more... the more...' structure to link physical comfort with the psychological state of being a child. By stating 'because you are a little girl,' I bypass the critical faculty and establish the regression as a present reality. 4. Sensory Engagement: Introduce tactile sensations (barefoot, water) to ground the trance in physical feeling, making the mental imagery more vivid and undeniable. 5. Permissive Uncertainty: Use 'I don't know...' and 'Maybe...' to reduce resistance, allowing the client's subconscious to fill in the specific details of their own experience.

rule3.text: It will move up in little jerks. Maybe you felt that jerk. It’s coming up. See that jerk?
rule3.reasoning: 먼저 내담자의 팔이 '작게 경련하며 올라갈 것'이라는 미래 지향적 전제를 설정하여 무의식적 기대를 형성합니다. 내담자가 고개를 저으며 부정적인 반응을 보일 때, 이를 논박하는 대신 '아마 그 경련을 느꼈을 것'이라며 감각적 경험을 부드럽게 재정의(Reframing)합니다. 내담자의 시선이 자신의 손으로 향하는 순간을 놓치지 않고 '그 경련이 보이나요?'라고 질문함으로써, 의식적인 저항을 뚫고 무의식적으로 일어나고 있는 신체적 현상에 주의를 고착시킵니다. 이는 내담자가 자신의 의지와 상관없이 몸이 움직이고 있다는 사실을 스스로 확인하게 만드는 전략적 관찰 기법입니다.
